name: Ricobayo Reservoir Loop
name_original: Muelas del Pan – vuelta por Alcañices – Embalse de Ricobayo
description: This difficult 60.8-mile road cycling route offers challenging terrain, scenic views of Ricobayo Reservoir, and impressive engineering marve
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Muelas Del Pan traverse a landscape characterized by verdant hillsides, cork oak forests, and the Esla River with its steep, rocky banks. The region features well-maintained roads that offer a tranquil environment for cyclists. A prominent natural feature is the Ricobayo Reservoir, an impressive hydroelectric project on the Esla River, providing scenic viewpoints along many routes. The terrain offers a mix of challenging ascents and serene stretches, suitable for various fitness levels. Best road cycling routes around Muelas Del Pan The most popular road cycling route is Circular by the west of Parque Natural Arribes del Duero — by the Salto de Villalcampo, a 21.9 miles (35.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 44 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the Arribes del Duero natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Zamora and Sayago Region Loop, a difficult 67.2 miles (108.2 km) path. This route explores the wider Zamora area, featuring varied landscapes and significant elevation gains. Local road cyclists also love the Ricobayo Reservoir Loop, a 60.8 miles (97.9 km) trail leading through the Ricobayo Reservoir area, often completed in about 4 hours 30 minutes. Road cycling around Muelas Del Pan is defined by verdant hillsides, the Esla River valley, and the Ricobayo Reservoir, with routes offering options for different ability levels. The routes in Muelas Del Pan are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.25 stars from more than 4 reviews. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Muelas Del Pan's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Muelas Del Pan?
A: There are over 25 road cycling routes around Muelas Del Pan, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include routes of varying difficulty, from moderate to difficult, ensuring options for different fitness levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Muelas Del Pan?
A: Road cycling in Muelas Del Pan features a varied landscape of verdant hillsides, cork oak forests, and the dramatic Esla River valley with its steep, rocky banks. Routes often include challenging ascents and serene stretches, with mostly well-paved surfaces on quiet minor roads, providing a tranquil environment for cyclists.

Q: What landmarks or viewpoints can I see while road cycling in Muelas Del Pan?
A: Many routes offer stunning vistas, particularly around the impressive Ricobayo Reservoir. You can also encounter engineering marvels like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5459672" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ricobayo Dam&lt;/a&gt; and the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3409877" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Requejo Bridge&lt;/a&gt;. For panoramic views, consider routes passing by viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5840033" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador del Hullón&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/8142834" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rodilla Campana Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any natural attractions to explore near the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in natural beauty, including the Esla River and its dramatic 'Arribes del Esla' depression. You might also find routes that pass close to the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5442792" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Abelón Waterfall&lt;/a&gt; or offer glimpses of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5441812" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arribes del Duero&lt;/a&gt; natural park, known for its deep canyons and diverse wildlife.

Q: What cultural or historical sites can I visit near Muelas Del Pan?
A: Beyond the natural landscapes, Muelas Del Pan itself features local and vernacular architecture, including the chapel of St. Stephen. The region is also only 22 kilometers from Zamora, known as the 'Pearl of the Romanesque' for its numerous Romanesque churches, offering a rich cultural excursion opportunity.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Muelas Del Pan?
A: The region offers enjoyable cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide the most pleasant weather with moderate temperatures. Summer can be warm, but early morning or late afternoon rides are still very popular. The quiet roads make it a good destination outside of peak tourist seasons.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Muelas Del Pan?
A: While many routes involve elevation gains, there are segments and quieter roads that can be suitable for families with older children or those comfortable with moderate distances. The region's tranquil environment and well-paved surfaces contribute to a pleasant experience. Always check the route's difficulty and elevation profile before setting out.

Q: Can I bring my dog on road cycling routes in Muelas Del Pan?
A: Road cycling routes are generally on public roads, so dogs are typically allowed if they are safely secured (e.g., in a trailer or basket). However, it's important to be mindful of local regulations and ensure your dog's safety and comfort, especially on longer rides or in warmer weather. The region is home to wildlife, so keeping dogs under control is essential.

Q: Is parking available for road cyclists in Muelas Del Pan?
A: Yes, Muelas Del Pan and surrounding villages typically offer public parking areas suitable for cyclists. Given the tranquil nature of the area, finding convenient parking near route starting points is generally straightforward, especially in smaller towns.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in the Muelas Del Pan area are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025434799/ricobayo-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ricobayo Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular option, as is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1369343944/circular-by-the-west-of-parque-natural-arribes-del-duero-by-the-salto-de-villalcampo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Circular by the west of Parque Natural Arribes del Duero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Muelas Del Pan?
A: The road cycling routes in Muelas Del Pan are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.25 stars from more than 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the tranquil environment, well-maintained roads, and the stunning natural beauty, particularly around the Ricobayo Reservoir and the Esla River valley.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay for cyclists in Muelas Del Pan?
A: Muelas Del Pan and nearby towns offer local establishments where cyclists can find refreshments and accommodation. You'll find traditional Spanish cafes and restaurants, as well as guesthouses or rural accommodations that cater to visitors exploring the region. It's always a good idea to check availability, especially during peak seasons.
tips:
The Ricobayo Dam is located on the Esla River, in the province of Zamora (Castilla y León), and is part of the Iberdrola hydroelectric system.
Completed in 1934, it was one of the major hydraulic engineering projects of the 20th century in Spain.
Small town located on the banks of the Aliste river, one of the tributaries of the Esla river. During drafts, the river dries up into a small stream.
The Valdoradas Bridge crosses the Esla River Reservoir on the ZA-911 road. During dry seasons, this section of the reservoir can dry up.

Bridges that cross the Esla river. Inaugurated in 2007, the new Manzanal del Barco bridge left in ruins the old and narrow bridge built during the construction of the Ricobayo Reservoir over the Esla River in 1931 until it was finally closed to traffic in 2017.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Muelas del Pan – return via Alcañices – Ricobayo Reservoir route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult, designed for experienced racebike cyclists with very good fitness. It features significant elevation changes, including over 1,000 meters of ascent, and some challenging sections that may require considerable effort.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this racebike route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily features well-paved asphalt and other paved surfaces, making it suitable for racebikes. However, be prepared for significant elevation changes and sections that can be exposed to wind. While mostly smooth, some more demanding segments in the broader area might include unpaved sections, so always check the route details carefully.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the Muelas del Pan – return via Alcañices – Ricobayo Reservoir route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable features and scenic spots. Highlights include the impressive Ricobayo Dam with its spectacular views, the historic Vegalatrave Church, the Valdoradas Bridge (Esla River Reservoir), and The Three Bridges of Manzanal del Barco. The route also offers picturesque landscapes overlooking the Ricobayo Reservoir and passes through charming rural towns.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during spring and autumn when temperatures are milder and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can be very hot in the Zamora province, making longer rides more challenging. Always check the local weather forecast before you go.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Muelas del Pan – return via Alcañices – Ricobayo Reservoir route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, you can typically find parking in the towns along the route, such as Muelas del Pan or near the Ricobayo Reservoir. Look for public parking spaces in these villages, keeping in mind that availability might vary.

faqs.question: Is the Muelas del Pan – return via Alcañices – Ricobayo Reservoir route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebiking on roads and paved surfaces. While dogs are generally allowed in public spaces, bringing a dog on a racebike tour of this distance and difficulty is not typically recommended for their safety or comfort. If you do bring a dog, ensure they are well-trained, can keep pace, and are always on a leash when off the bike, especially in populated areas or near traffic.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need any special permits or have to pay entrance fees to cycle the Muelas del Pan – return via Alcañices – Ricobayo Reservoir route. It traverses public roads and areas.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of the Camino Portugués de la vía de la Plata and the Mtb Camino Portugués de la vía de la Plata, covering about 11% of the route. It also touches upon smaller segments of the Cordel de Zamora a Sanabria.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through several rural towns like Muelas del Pan, Alcañices, Villaseco del Pan, Pino del Oro, Villadepera, and Bermillo de Sayago. These towns typically offer local cafes, restaurants, and sometimes small guesthouses or rural accommodations where you can refuel and rest.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a full day of cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length (~98 km) and difficulty, you should bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire for varying weather conditions, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device or the Komoot app with the downloaded tour is highly recommended for navigation.

faqs.question: Are there any particularly challenging sections or insider tips for this route?
faqs.answer: The area is known for its challenging cycling, with some local routes even featuring sections referred to as "carretera de los infiernos" (hell's road). While this specific Komoot tour is difficult, be prepared for sustained climbs and potential exposure to wind, especially around the Ricobayo Reservoir. Pacing yourself and conserving energy for the latter parts of the ride is a good strategy.

waypoints.name: The Three Bridges of Manzanal del Barco
